evidence: The problem asks for monochromatic solutions to the unit-fraction equation 1/a = 1/b + 1/c under any finite colouring of the integers.

evidence: Asking for a monochromatic solution in any finite colouring of the integers is a Ramsey-theoretic statement (Brown-Rodl, 'Monochromatic solutions to equations with unit fractions').

evidence: States every positive integer is an Egyptian fraction whose denominators are each products of three distinct primes.

evidence: Asks for two finite sets of primes P, Q with (sum_{p in P} 1/p)(sum_{q in Q} 1/q) = 1.

evidence: The equation is a product of two sums of unit fractions 1/p equalling 1; erdosproblems.com tags the problem 'unit fractions'.
verify_note: product of sums of reciprocals of primes = unit fractions, load-bearing; QID live

evidence: The weakened version still asks for products of sums of unit fractions equal to 1, over pairwise-coprime sets excluding 1.

evidence: The barrier states any solution over sets of primes P, Q with Q nonempty uses at least 59 primes and prod P >= 2*10^56.

evidence: The theorem asks for a set B of density 0 (B.HasDensity 0) such that A+B contains all but finitely many integers.

evidence: The conclusion is about the sumset A+B (Pointwise) covering all sufficiently large integers; Lorentz [Lo54], 'On a problem of additive number theory'.
